description: We are excited to announce that we will be hosting our first ever Trivia Night 🌌🤖🦉 by the Physics GSO 🚀. Please come along to join our exciting night filled with fun questions and great company. At the end of the event there will be prizes included for first, second and third place. Bring your friends to come along and join our annual event of the year, trivia night. 👾 There will be free food as usual, and please RSVP in advance so we have a good estimate for how much food to order.

description: Join us for an exciting Cognitive Science Career Conversations event, co-hosted by the Cognitive Science Club and the Rutgers University Center for Cognitive Science. This session, “What Works and What Doesn’t: Things to Know to Land an Internship,” will feature Elizabeth Langemak, Associate Director of Campus Programs at Verizon, and Rania Plazas, Senior Assistant Director of Rutgers Internship & Co-op Programs. Taking place on April 13, 2026 from 5:30–7:00 PM in Voorhees Hall 104 (College Ave Campus), this event will offer real, practical insights into navigating the internship search, standing out as a candidate, and avoiding common mistakes. Whether you're just starting to explore opportunities or actively applying, this is a great chance to gain insider advice, ask questions, and connect with professionals who want to help you succeed.
name: What Works and What Doesn’t- Things to know to land an internship

description: Register to attend! Please RSVP even if you cannot attend and we will send you information! Dimension of Wellness: SOCIAL & EMOTIONAL Join us for Raíces y Comunidad, a Latinx Graduate Student Gathering centered on connection, community, and wellness. Hosted at the Center for Latino Arts and Culture, this gathering is an opportunity to unwind, connect with fellow graduate students, and get to know a cultural space on campus that is here for you. Throughout the evening, you will enjoy good food and have the chance to meet staff from the Center for Latino Arts and Culture and the Office of Graduate Student Life and learn about resources and opportunities available to support you. Children are welcome. This event is part of Mental Health & Wellness Week and will include empanadas, arepas and desert from Merey's Venezuelan Restaurant, a mini wellness station, community reflection wall, and music! Come as you are, bring a friend, and take a moment to recharge in community.  First 40 students will receive swag from the Office of Graduate of Student Life!
location: Center for Latino Arts and Culture

You can take your rock with you as a reminder of your resilience or leave it behind to inspire others in the graduate community.  Hosted by Megan Lotts, Art Librarian.

description: In recognition of National Minority Health Awareness Month, campus and community partners are coming together to host a Sickle Cell Awareness Blood Drive in collaboration with the New York Blood Center. We encourage the Rutgers community to come out and donate! This is a great opportunity to make a real difference by strengthening the local blood supply and supporting those living with Sickle Cell Disease, which disproportionately affects minority communities. Stop by to learn more about sickle cell and make a lifesaving donation! All donors will receive free snacks, a $10 Tango e-gift card, a free Rutgers Cinema ticket and are eligible to receive volunteer hours.  Walk-ins are welcome, but registration encouraged. description: Join Rutgers Center for Nonprofit Leadership and Development and Rutgers SPAA Office of Community Engagement for Reimagining Democracy: Justice, Reentry & Community Voice Through Film, an evening of film and dialogue exploring issues of incarceration, reentry, and community voice. The program will feature a screening of the documentary “A Woman on the Outside,” followed by a moderated discussion with the film’s producer, Kiara C. Jones, and representatives from New Jersey Scholarship and Transformative Education in Prisons (NJ STEP). Together, we will reflect on the role of storytelling, lived experience, and education in shaping more just and inclusive communities. This event is part of Rutgers–Newark’s Solving Grand Challenges Month and is presented in partnership with the Rutgers Democracy Lab.   Please click here to register
name: Reimagining Democracy: Justice Reentry & Community Voice Through Film: Newark

description: Featuring Dr. Cymie Payne, SEED's Environmental Policy Extravaganza will allow students to both learn about and actively engage with New Jersey’s environmental policies that are currently open for public comment.  Dr. Payne, Chair of the Ocean Law Specialist Group of the International Union for Conservation of Nature’s World Commission on Environmental Law, will lead a special guest lecture on how environmental rulemaking works and how individuals can meaningfully participate in the process. You may read more about her bio here. Following the lecture, participants will take part in an interactive group activity where we will draft and submit our own public comments on active environmental legislation. This is a unique chance to turn knowledge into action. In an era shaped by online misinformation and growing eco-anxiety, it’s more important than ever to use our voices to advocate for real, lasting change. This event will teach you where to find proposed rules, how to submit effective public comments, and what language can help your voice stand out in the policymaking process. Pizza will be provided!   Wednesdays, 8:00pm-9:00pm Room 211 (Concepts Lab), Richard Weeks, Busch Campus
name: 4/15/26 SEED Environmental Policy Extravganza!

description: Sam Schwartz Transportation Research Program: The Inaugural Future of Transportation Conference — Autonomous Vehicles: the Benefits, the Risks, and the Need for Regulation    When?       Thursday, April 16, 2026, 2:15 pm to 5:30 pm       Where?       Roosevelt House @ Hunter College (Upper East Side, NYC) - also virtual option       How to get there?       Take NJT to Penn Station, walk to Herald Square on 6th Ave, and take the N/Q/R/W to 5th Ave / 59 St or Lexington Ave / 63rd stations.       How to register?       https://host.nxt.blackbaud.com/registration-form/?formId=78405bda-b596-448e-95c2-660a68f479ab&envId=p-rzKFBb2xVk253Ulcjd_x1w&zone=usa (in-person or virtual)       How much?       Free!       Reimbursement?       Free ticket, but we will apply for travel / dinner funding!       Why?      (copied from Artem): “Hi everyone, Roosevelt House and Sam Schwartz (the guy who coined term gridlock and former NYC transportation commissioner and consultant) are doing this transportation conference focused on AVs, thought it might be of interest to someone”     New York Transit Museum - Interborough Express MTA talk    When?       Thursday, April 16, 2026, 6:00 pm to 7:30 pm (time for chatting with presenters and other attendees afterwards)       Where?       New York Transit Museum       How to get there?       The best way from New Brunswick is usually taking NJT to Penn Station and taking the A/C to Jay St-Metrotech. We can go together from New Brunswick!       How to register?     Get your $15 ticket here       How much?       Member tickets are $10; if you want to get your own it’s $15. NJT plus subway round trip is $39.10.       Reimbursement?       Yes, as long as GSA approves the request! Save your NJT / MTA tickets and receipts, and your ticket email and proof of payment (if you got your own).       Why?      Transit Museum talks are usually short but informative! A lot of transit nerds and professionals (especially MTA) attend from the NYC area, and the presenters usually stick around to answer questions after. Plus, the IBX is a cool project and I’ve heard that the people working on it are very receptive to hearing new ideas.
name: Hunter College Autonomous Vehicles Conference / New York Transit Museum Interborough Express
organizationName: Bloustein Enthusiasts and Advocates for Transportation

description: Want to get more confident speaking in class, working in teams, or handling disagreements without things getting awkward? Join Improv for Democracy — a fun, interactive workshop where you’ll practice real communication skills through improv (no acting experience needed).   🗓 Friday, April 17 | 1:15–2:30 PM📍 Labor Education Center Auditorium🍕 + 🥗 Free lunch available at 12:30 pm REGISTER NOW! Space is limited.   You’ll learn how to: 🎯 Speak up without overthinking🎯 Handle different opinions with confidence🎯 Think on your feet in discussions and group work🎯 Communicate more effectively (great for interviews too) ✨ Great experiential learning activity for resumes and leadership development.   No prep. No pressure. Just show up and participate. Bring a friend — it’ll be fun, practical, and way more interactive than a typical lecture. REGISTER NOW! Space is limited.   Co-sponsored by: School of Management and Labor Relations, The Institute of Inclusive Pedagogy, the Edward J. Bloustein School of Planning and Public Policy, the School of Social Work, the Mason Gross School of Arts, the Graduate School of Education, Rutgers Business School, and the School of Communication and Information. The event is supported by Rutgers Democracy Lab and is a part of the Solving Grand Challenges Month.

description: The First Annual Conference: Language at Crossroads invites scholars, graduate students, and researchers to contribute to an interdisciplinary dialogue on language across its multiple levels of analysis. The conference will feature four panels: panel 1: phonetics and phonology, panel 2: morphology and syntax, panel 3: semantics and pragmatics, and panel 4: project launch. Each panel is open to contributions engaging with these levels of language, broadly conceived. Thus, contributions to neurolinguistics, cognitive sciences, psychology, psycholinguistics, sociolinguistics, discourse analysis, education, cultural studies, and related fields. Abstract Submission: here by March 20th Register: here by April 3rd
name: 1st Annual Conference: Language at Crossroads

description: ***[PLEASE NOTE THE NEW DATE!]*** Please join SPCR for the workshop "The Things We Hold" to explore the experiences of working mothers, the multitasking, the invisible labor, and the things we carry in our daily lives. This workshop will be kid-friendly, and participants will create a bag and fill it with the things they carry. Refreshments and beverages are also provided! *Spaces are limited to 12 families (12 RSVPs). Please RSVP to register! “The Things We Hold” is a creative, reflective workshop that invites mothers and children to explore what they carry in their lives, both tangible and invisible. Inspired by Ursula K. Le Guin’s idea that the first human tool might have been a bag, participants create personalized “carrier bags” filled with objects, memories, feelings, responsibilities, dreams, and routines. Through drawing, writing, decorating, and collaborative conversation, families reflect on what sustains, burdens, or delights them. The process encourages curiosity, empathy, and dialogue, prompting questions like “Who carries this more?” or “Is this heavy or light?” The workshop concludes with a sharing circle and portrait documentation, offering space to acknowledge both the visible and invisible parts of our daily lives, and to consider what we might wish to carry less of.
name: [NEW DATE!!] “The Things We Hold” Workshop BY SPCR
organizationName: Supporting Parents and Caregivers at Rutgers (Graduate Organization)
organizationNames: Graduate Student Life (Department at Rutgers University)
Supporting Parents and Caregivers at Rutgers (Graduate Organization)

description: Please join us on April 18, 2026 for the Rutgers Tree Planting Festival, where we will be planting 3000 trees using the Miyawaki Method in place of an abandoned roadway that was removed last year by Rutgers Institutional Planning and Operations. There will be trees, art, and music!  This event is open to all members of the Rutgers community!  Sign up for the 10:00am-12:00pm, 12:00pm-2:00pm, or 2:00pm-5:00pm. (But you can come and go as you please!)

description: Come join us on Sunday, April 19, for Haru's biggest event of the semester, Spring 2026 Showcase! Our goal is to share Korean pop culture through the art of dance. This event is open to everyone: general members, families, Rutgers students, and non-Rutgers students! Our showcase is our biggest event, where our members get to showcase a multitude of K-pop performances they’ve been preparing for months for our audience. There are two acts with an intermission, which includes a raffle with a bunch of prizes our audience can partake in and possibly win big! And the best part is that admission is FREE for all!  You don't have to be performing to attend, so come out and support our performers! ♡ Let's cheer each other on and enjoy each other's company! You may also get some sweet treats, snacks, and refreshments from us to enjoy while watching performances 👀 Our showcase is also livestreamed on our YouTube page for audience members who cannot attend in person, but would love to support our performers! If you're watching from online, thank you for supporting Haru Dance ♡ More information on parking will be detailed here later in the semester. Contact us directly on IG: @harudance_official or Email: haru.kdcc@gmail.com if you have any questions! Can't wait to see y'all soon!
location: Cook/Douglass - Trayes Hall Douglass Student Center
name: HARU Spring 2026 Showcase

description: CityNerd Live at Hunter College    When?       Friday, April 24, 2026, 6:00 pm to 9:00 pm       Where?       Hunter College Assembly Hall       How to get there?       The best way from New Brunswick is usually taking NJT to Penn Station and taking the E to the 6. Should take roughly 1.5 hours.       How to register?       Get your $20 ticket here       How much?       Tickets are $20. Travel is $33.10 round trip on NJT and $6.00 round trip on the subway.       Reimbursement?       Yes, as long as GSA approves the request! Save your NJT / MTA tickets and receipts, and your ticket email and proof of payment (if you got your own).       Why?      CityNerd makes entertaining videos (at least imo), and I’m sure it will be a fun discussion. Betsy Plum (Riders Alliance) and Tiffany-Ann Taylor (Regional Plan Association) are also there.
name: CityNerd Live at Hunter College
organizationName: Bloustein Enthusiasts and Advocates for Transportation

description: 2026 North Jersey Transit Forum    What?       Join the North Jersey Transit Forum and engage in meaningful discussions on urban planning and transportation policies. This event brings together stakeholders, transportation agencies, government, nonprofits, educators and the business community to develop a common vision for the future of transit and mobility in North Jersey and the Tri-State region.       When?       Saturday, April 25, 2026, 8:00 am to 6:00 pm  7:15am     Doors open, Registration & Breakfast 8:00am   Keynote, talks & panels 12:30pm   Lunch 1:45pm   Infrastructure Tours Depart 4:30pm  Afternoon Tea & Coffee 5:00pm   Closing Remarks 6:00pm   Happy Hour        Where?       Mac Mahon Student Center - The Duncan Family Sky Room - Saint Peter's University - 47 Glenwood Ave, Jersey City, NJ 07306       How to get there?       Congregate at New Brunswick station at 6:20 AM, leave on the 6:27 AM NEC to NY Penn, change to PATH, arrive at Journal Square around 7:45 AM.       How to register?       Get your $45 student ticket here       Reimbursement?       Yes, as long as GSA approves the request! Save your NJT / PATH tickets and receipts, and your ticket email and proof of payment (if you got your own).       Why?      Many high-up politicians, transit professionals, and advocates will be present. Talks will include first/last-mile transit, capital programs, better PATH and light rail, and BRT. Sounds like a lot of educational and networking opportunities!
location: Mac Mahon Student Center - The Duncan Family Sky Room - Saint Peter's University
name: 2026 North Jersey Transit Forum
organizationName: Bloustein Enthusiasts and Advocates for Transportation

description: The 2026 Graduate Research in Interdisciplinary Biosciences (GRIB) Conference aims to understand how modern environments interact with natural biological systems, and how these interactions shape metabolic health, neuropsychiatric function, and overall human well-being. On May 6, 2026, GRIB will convene at ARH-200 on the Cook/Douglass campus, bringing together perspectives from the biological, psychological, and social sciences. The conference will:  Dr. Vogel will examine humanity’s evolutionary foundations, with emphasis on the environmental and metabolic conditions that shaped human biology. Dr. LeDoux will investigate the neural basis of emotion and subjective experience, and how modern contexts influence and at times disrupt these processes. Dena Seidel will explore pathways toward greater alignment between individuals, societies, and their environments, highlighting opportunities for better communicating innovation in health, culture, and design.  Participants are invited to engage with a shared aim: to understand how scientific progress can support health span in populations and foster lives that are more coherent, adaptive, and meaningful, with a greater sense of integration within the modern landscape.
name: Graduate Research in Interdisciplinary Biosciences (GRIB) Conference - Relationship Between the Modern Environment and Human Biology
organizationNames: Department of Plant Biology (Cook Researchers of Plant Science) (Graduate Organization)
